# Transcrição: WFF2019_M6_L04-3_Make_Sure_They_See_You_MP4

## [00:00 - 03:03]

Hi, this is Don Crowther with the video about making sure they actually see you during your webinar. So one of the key things you need to do during your webinar is to build trust and a great way to do that is to make sure they see your face on their screen. So here's my recommendation. For at least 15 minutes of your webinar, interspersed throughout the webinar, you should stop sharing your slides, put your face on the screen and just talk with them. I do realize that some webinar services just don't have this built in. And so if yours, the one that you're using doesn't do that, don't worry about this. But you also may want to consider changing webinar suppliers so that you do have one that does allow you to do that. So let's talk about some places in the webinar script that it makes a lot of sense to do this. I like to do it right out of the gate, the very beginning. So actually what I'd like to do is I'd like to have a slide on the screen that shows the title slide for the webinar. And then as soon as I start, I turn that slide off. They're seeing my face. I'm saying hi. I'm talking to doing the introductory section without them seeing slides. And then when I get into the credibility part of the intro, then I go back to sharing the screen and I show some slides because that credibility helps them during that. And then at least two times during the content portion of the webinar itself, I ask them for their feedback and experiences. And I will have my face on camera during that time as I'm giving them feedback and listening and playing back to them their feedback that they're giving me. I also like to run the Q&A session with my face on camera. That's probably one of the most important times for them to see it because oftentimes some of those Q&As, those questions are actually objections and challenges. I want them to have the connection of seeing my face during that period of time. Now realize going from sharing your screen to not sharing your screen is a second. And so you can go back and forth. And so as you're going along with your questions, if you've got something that you want to show something on screen or you want to re show them your URL or show them what they get in the product or whatever, just share your screen again. There you are. And then at the end of that, unshare your screen and there's your face on the screen again. It's not a big deal to go back and forth. And so do it frequently. Another point is during the emotional close. Usually if you look at this list, the times when I want my face to be on camera is the times when I'm really working on emotions with them. With one small exception. And that is the second level benefits. I want to have slides on the screen for that. But other times when I'm trying to get connection and emotional connection build up, that's when

## [03:03 - 03:46]

I want to have my face on the screen. And I suggest that you frequently jump back and forth between slides and your face on camera. Now, I also realize that some webinar services also allow you to have your face on camera in a corner while showing slides. I don't really have a problem with that as long as you design your slide deck so that you never have words that that picture is covering up. But even with doing that, I would suggest you still turn off slide and get your face on camera full screen during the key parts of your webinar when you're doing the most work to try to develop that relationship and trust with them.
